coredata.dc:title: Study on the evolution of internal resistance and entropy-thermal coefficients during the aging process of lithium-ion traction batteries
coredata.prism:publicationName: e-Prime - Advances in Electrical Engineering, Electronics and Energy
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Ma, Tianyi
Sun, Zhipeng
Han, Ce
Wang, Yingzhou
Zhang, Baoqiang
Zhang, Jinjie
Chen, Liduo
Dai, Xiaoqian
Zhang, Shijie
Shen, Shaopeng
coredata.dc:description: The relationship between internal resistance and entropy-thermal coefficients significantly influences the thermal behavior and performance of traction batteries. This study systematically investigates four types of traction batteries under varying state of charge (SOC) (0 %–100 %) and temperature conditions (–20 °C to 55 °C), analyzing their entropy-thermal coefficients and internal resistance variations. Experimental results reveal that entropy-thermal coefficients exhibit non-linear changes with SOC, where values range from –1.2 mV/K to 2.8 mV/K, directly impacting heat generation. Additionally, internal resistance varies significantly with SOC and temperature, increasing by over 300 % in low-temperature conditions compared to room temperature. These findings highlight the necessity of adaptive thermal management strategies considering both internal resistance and entropy-thermal coefficients, provide new insights for optimizing battery thermal behavior and lifespan by integrating dynamic entropy-thermal models into battery management systems.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Lithium-ion batteries
Internal resistance
Entropy-thermal coefficients
State of health

Temperature-Internal Resistance curves of the battery samples. (a) h-NCM, (b) m-NCM, (c) Si-C, (d) LFP.
Battery entropy-thermal coefficient under different temperature and SOC (a) h-NCM, (b) m-NCM, (c) Si-C, (d) LFP.
Horizontal comparison of entropy-thermal coefficient of batteries in different chemistry.
Internal resistance of the battery varies with temperature under different SOC-SOH conditions.
Entropy-thermal coefficient of the battery varies with temperature under different SOC-SOH conditions.

head.ce:title: Study on the evolution of internal resistance and entropy-thermal coefficients during the aging process of lithium-ion traction batteries

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Authors contributed equally in this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The relationship between internal resistance and entropy-thermal coefficients significantly influences the thermal behavior and performance of traction batteries. This study systematically investigates four types of traction batteries under varying state of charge (SOC) (0 %–100 %) and temperature conditions (–20 °C to 55 °C), analyzing their entropy-thermal coefficients and internal resistance variations. Experimental results reveal that entropy-thermal coefficients exhibit non-linear changes with SOC, where values range from –1.2 mV/K to 2.8 mV/K, directly impacting heat generation. Additionally, internal resistance varies significantly with SOC and temperature, increasing by over 300 % in low-temperature conditions compared to room temperature. These findings highlight the necessity of adaptive thermal management strategies considering both internal resistance and entropy-thermal coefficients, provide new insights for optimizing battery thermal behavior and lifespan by integrating dynamic entropy-thermal models into battery management systems.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Lithium-ion batteries are widely used in electric vehicles and various portable rechargeable devices [1–3]. The performance of lithium-ion batteries is influenced by various factors owing to their electrochemical characteristics [4,5], which significantly affect their lifespan and performance. In practical applications, battery performance varies with its states of health (SOH) in response to parameters such as temperature and internal resistance [6–8]. Lithium-ion batteries perform optimally within a temperature range of 0 °C–40 °C. At lower temperatures, reduced lithium-ion activity increases internal resistance, leading to diminished discharge capacity and lower energy output [9,10]. Charging at low temperatures can lead to lithium metal deposition and dendrite formation, which increases the risks of separator puncture and internal short circuits [11–13]. In contrast, a moderate increase in temperature can enhance electrolyte conductivity and temporarily improve battery output power and charge–discharge performance [14]. However, excessive temperatures accelerate side reactions within the battery, disrupt internal chemical equilibrium, and significantly reduce its lifespan [15–17]. Therefore, investigating temperature and internal resistance variations in lithium-ion batteries is crucial for effectively analyzing and predicting performance thresholds and lifespan [18–20].

Typically, the Arrhenius equation is used to examine the reaction rate within a battery [21,22]. The reaction rate k is expressed as follows:
(1)
k = A
e
−
E a
R T

Here, A represents the pre-exponential factor, E denotes the activation energy, R indicates the gas constant, and T signifies the absolute temperature. The Arrhenius equation can effectively describe the relationship between charge-transfer resistance and temperature in traction batteries. Generally, as temperature increases, the charge-transfer rate in the electrolyte and electrode materials accelerates, leading to a decrease in charge-transfer resistance [23,24]. Conversely, as the temperature decreases, the electrochemical reaction rate slows down, resulting in a significant increase in charge-transfer resistance.

To investigate the temperature variations in lithium-ion traction batteries, it is crucial to analyze their heat generation, which mainly involves reversible and irreversible heat processes [25]. The total heat generation is typically described using the Bernardi heat generation rate equation [26,27]:
(2)
Q =
I V
[
(
E −
E 0
)
+ T
d
E 0
d T
]

Here, Q represents the total heat generation, I denotes the charge/discharge current of the battery, V indicates the battery volume, E denotes the terminal voltage, E0 signifies the open-circuit voltage, T denotes the battery temperature, and
d
E 0
d T
represents the entropy-thermal coefficient. The two components on the right side of the equation represent the irreversible and reversible heat of the battery. The entropy-thermal coefficient is a key parameter for characterizing the relationship between the voltage plateau and temperature in lithium-ion batteries [28,29]. Under varying conditions of temperature and state of charge (SOC), the entropy-thermal coefficient can influence internal resistance. In the dynamic operation of lithium-ion batteries, the entropy-thermal coefficient and internal resistance have interrelated effects. The reversible heat dominated by the entropy-thermal coefficient and the irreversible heat dominated by internal resistance both influence the battery temperature, and their effects are correlated through temperature. When the entropy-thermal coefficient is negative, the reversible heat partially offsets the heat generated by internal resistance, suppressing the temperature rise; conversely, when it is positive, it exacerbates the temperature rise. The temperature change of the battery directly affects the internal resistance, forming an indirect correlation chain of "entropy-thermal coefficient → temperature → internal resistance." Meanwhile, both the entropy-thermal coefficient and internal resistance vary significantly with SOC. In high SOC ranges, the entropy-thermal coefficient is typically positive, and the diffusion rate of lithium ions in the electrodes decreases, leading to an increase in polarization resistance. Temperature and SOC act as common state variables, causing their dynamic responses to exhibit coupling characteristics.

Therefore, investigating both the temperature-entropy variation and internal resistance of lithium-ion batteries is crucial. During the discharge process of lithium-ion batteries, the heat generation primarily includes reaction heat, ohmic resistance heat, polarization heat, and heat from side reactions. Among these, ohmic resistance heat and polarization heat are parts of irreversible reactions [30,31], while reaction heat is associated with chemical reactions. The magnitude of internal resistance directly influences the battery's heat generation, thereby affecting the entropy-thermal coefficient. As the state of health (SOH) of the battery declines during aging, the irreversible heat caused by internal resistance increases, affecting the proportion of heat generation in the battery. This indicates that the entropy-thermal coefficient is closely related to the battery's health status and internal resistance. Studying the entropy-thermal and internal resistance changes in lithium-ion batteries is beneficial for researching their aging mechanisms and analyzing lifecycle evolution [32,33].

Researchers have extensively investigated the entropy-thermal coefficient of lithium-ion batteries. Experimental studies have explored the relationship between SOC and internal resistance under varying conditions. Statistical theories have been applied to mathematically analyze the entropy-thermal coefficient and provide detailed insights into its properties. In non-destructive analysis, thermodynamic parameters have been used to highlight the significance of the entropy-thermal coefficient in research [19,20,31]. Moreover, statistical analyses have been conducted on the entropy changes in lithium-ion batteries under different states throughout their cycle life. Additionally, comparative studies have examined the heat generation of batteries under various cycling conditions. Furthermore, advanced analytical methods have been introduced to evaluate the entropy-thermal coefficient, particularly in heat generation in lithium nickel cobalt manganese oxide (NCM) batteries [17].

The entropy change of lithium-ion batteries plays a crucial role in determining their performance and stability. Early research primarily concentrated on comparing entropy variations across different electrode materials, with the goal of optimizing material compositions and leveraging the complementary properties of cathodes and anodes. This approach aimed to minimize entropy changes within full cells, thereby improving thermodynamic stability. In recent years, significant efforts have been devoted to developing more accurate methods for measuring entropy and its changes, addressing the limitations of traditional techniques such as long testing cycles and poor measurement precision. Additionally, there has been growing interest in using entropy as a tool to optimize battery management systems.

In theory, the entropy-thermal coefficient and internal resistance are influenced by both the composition of traction batteries and their SOH, which are critical factors to consider in research and analysis. These two parameters can serve as dynamic indicators under typical operating conditions. Therefore, elucidating the relationship between the entropy-thermal coefficient, internal resistance, and their dependencies on the composition and SOH of traction batteries is a key aspect of performance analysis.

Under different systems, the entropy-thermal coefficients exhibit changes with SOC, and these changes are nonlinear, varying with different systems. Therefore, the fixed assignment method of entropy-thermal coefficients in some existing models that construct thermoelectric coupling cannot reflect the actual differences in thermal phenomena of lithium-ion batteries at different SOC states. This also increases the error when performing heat generation calculations. Additionally, as the battery ages with the decline of SOH, internal changes such as the decrease in active materials, side reactions like lithium plating, and degradation of the structure of both anode and cathode materials will alter the entropy-thermal relationship of the battery. Using a fixed value assignment method will also increase the error. Therefore, the entropy-thermal coefficient of the battery needs to be calculated based on the actual SOC and SOH states of the battery, rather than using a fixed value assignment method. However, no systematic experimental data currently exist to support related trend analyses. In this study, we conduct a comprehensive analysis of the relationship between internal resistance and entropy-thermal coefficients across different traction battery types under varying SOC and temperature conditions. The experimental approach involves multiple test scenarios, providing significant insights into battery performance and heat generation. By introducing the concept of entropy-thermal coefficients, we propose a novel framework for understanding battery thermal behavior. This work not only enhances the understanding of internal resistance and entropy-thermal interactions but also lays the foundation for more efficient thermal management strategies, offering new avenues for improving battery lifespan and safety in real-world applications..

Four types of batteries with different compositions were selected for this study: high-nickel NCM (8:1:1)/graphite, medium-nickel NCM (1:1:1)/graphite, medium-nickel NCM (1:1:1)/silicon-carbon, and lithium iron phosphate/graphite. These batteries were denoted as h-NCM, m-NCM, Si-C, and LFP. Photographs of the four samples are shown in Fig. 1 .

The testing procedure is as follows:
a.
Three 1C standard charge–discharge cycles are performed on the sample to activate the battery.
b.
The battery is placed in a temperature chamber set to 25 °C ± 2 °C. SOC is adjusted to 100 % using the 1C charging method, and the temperature of the chamber is then increased at a rate of 1 °C/min until it reaches 45 °C. Afterward, the battery is allowed to stabilize at 45 °C, with a temperature variation rate of <1 °C/h. Once stabilized, the battery is considered to have reached thermal equilibrium.
c.
The temperature and internal resistance of the battery are measured repeatedly until the difference between two consecutive readings is <0.1 %, indicating that the battery has stabilized.
d.
The temperature of the battery is reduced to 40 °C at a rate not exceeding 1 °C/min. Once thermal equilibrium is achieved, step c is repeated. Testing proceeds at 5 °C intervals until the temperature reaches −20 °C.
e.
At 25 °C ± 2 °C, the battery is allowed to stabilize until thermal equilibrium is achieved. Subsequently, SOC is adjusted to 80 %, 60 %, 40 %, 20 %, and 0 % using a 0.5 C current. Steps c to d are repeated for each SOC condition until testing is completed for all temperature and SOC levels.

This study analyzes the voltage and internal resistance of the battery and calculates the entropy-thermal coefficient to assess the impact of different battery compositions and SOH on these parameters.

The entropy-thermal relationship tests were conducted on batteries with different compositions to examine the relationship between temperature and internal resistance (Fig. 2a –d). In the −20 °C–0 °C range, temperature exerts a greater impact on internal resistance than in other temperature ranges, leading to more pronounced fluctuations in internal resistance. In the 0 °C–15 °C range, both temperature and SOC have minimal effects on internal resistance, resulting in significantly smaller differences in internal resistance across temperatures compared with the −20 °C–0 °C range. Additionally, SOC variations exhibit a weaker impact on internal resistance in the 0 °C–15 °C range compared with the −20 °C–0 °C range. In the 15 °C–45 °C range, temperature has a less pronounced impact on internal resistance.

The underlying factors contributing to the increase in internal resistance can be elucidated as follows. As the temperature decreases, the viscosity of the electrolyte increases, which hinders lithium-ion migration and increases internal resistance. Under low-temperature conditions, the electrochemical reaction rate decreases, leading to a rapid increase in polarization resistance and the total internal resistance of the battery. Additionally, at lower temperatures, the resistance of internal battery components (including electrode active materials and electrolytes) increases, further contributing to the overall increase in the internal resistance of the battery. This effect is particularly pronounced in m-NCM batteries. At −20 °C, the internal resistance can exceed 300 mΩ. At temperatures exceeding 0 °C, the internal resistance rapidly decreases to ∼20 mΩ.

Regarding battery SOC across different systems, high-SOC batteries exhibit higher internal resistance than low-SOC batteries at low temperatures. Conversely, at high temperatures, low-SOC batteries feature higher internal resistance than high-SOC batteries. This indicates that the relationship between internal resistance and SOC symmetrically shifts on either side of a specific temperature point. In the normal and high-temperature range (15 °C–40 °C) during lithium-ion battery operations, high-SOC batteries exhibit relatively high electrolyte conductivity, which reduces the ohmic resistance. Additionally, high-SOC batteries exhibit lower charge-transfer impedance, further contributing to a reduction in direct current internal resistance. These factors result in lower internal resistance in lithium-ion batteries at high SOC levels compared with low SOC levels.

At low SOC levels, the electrolyte conductivity of the battery decreases owing to significant discharge, leading to an increase in internal resistance. This indicates that lithium-ion batteries exhibit lower internal resistance at higher SOC levels compared with lower SOC levels. Under low-temperature conditions, the viscosity of the electrolyte increases, which hinders the movement of charged ions, thereby reducing their mobility both within the electrolyte and through the separator and solid electrolyte interphase (SEI) layer. Consequently, the overall internal resistance of batteries across different systems increases. At low temperatures, the decrease in the electronic and ionic conductivity of the electrolyte outweighs the resistance-reducing effects of lower ohmic resistance and transfer impedance associated with higher SOC. Therefore, batteries exhibit higher internal resistance at higher SOC and low temperatures, and this phenomenon becomes more pronounced as the temperature decreases.

At −20 °C, the internal resistance of h-NCM, m-NCM, Si-C and LFP batteries at 100 % SOC is 26.928 mΩ, 245.68 mΩ, 23.548 mΩ, 1.3628 mΩ, respectively; the internal resistance at 0 SOC is 16.104 mΩ, 136.27 mΩ, 13.814 mΩ, 0.8352 mΩ, respectively. This variation in internal resistance with different SOC is particularly more significant than the differences observed in normal and high-temperature ranges. At low temperatures, storing batteries at a lower SOC can significantly reduce internal resistance. However, in normal and high-temperature ranges, a higher SOC can reduce internal resistance and increase the risk of overcharging and thermal instability. Owing to the minimal impact of SOC on internal resistance in these temperature ranges, maintaining the battery within a safe and appropriate SOC range is highly effective for ensuring its safety and prolonging its lifespan.

The entropy heat coefficient (Eq. (2)) was applied to analyze the voltage changes in different battery systems at various temperature points. Moreover, distribution maps of the entropy heat coefficient were generated for each temperature point and SOC level (Fig. 3a –d).

The data in Fig. 3 reveal that at 60 % SOC, h-NCM and m-NCM batteries exhibit the highest entropy-thermal coefficient. For other SOC levels, the coefficient increases progressively from low to high SOC. Similarly, the entropy-thermal coefficient of Si–C batteries gradually increases with increasing SOC. At both 60 % SOC and 100 % SOC, LFP batteries exhibit relatively high entropy-thermal coefficients. Additionally, at these SOC levels, the entropy-thermal coefficients remain nearly constant across different temperature conditions. A comprehensive comparison of entropy-thermal coefficients across all battery systems on the same scale is provided in Fig. 4 .

A comparison of the entropy-thermal coefficients across different battery systems reveals that m-NCM and LFP batteries exhibit a more uniform distribution of entropy-thermal coefficients compared with h-NCM and Si-C batteries. For h-NCM and Si–C batteries, the entropy-thermal coefficient varies significantly between lower SOC (SOC < 50 %) and higher SOC levels (SOC > 50 %). Moreover, the entropy-thermal coefficient of Si–C batteries significantly varies at temperatures above 35 °C.

During electrochemical reactions, higher entropy-thermal coefficients lead to increased heat generation (Eq. (2)), which increases the energy consumption of the battery and reduces its efficiency. Excessive heat generation can result in higher operating temperatures, which accelerates battery aging and increases the risk of prolonged high-temperature conditions, further exacerbating the risk of thermal instability.

To investigate the aging behavior of batteries with different health states, LFP batteries underwent long-cycle testing, and their SOH was adjusted to 100 %, 80 %, and 60 % to examine changes in the entropy-thermal coefficient during the aging process. The relationship between the entropy-thermal coefficient and internal resistance was analyzed, and SOH-resistance-temperature curves were established for a 100 % SOC–0 % SOC range (Fig. 5 ).

Similarly, the internal resistance of lithium-ion batteries decreases gradually as the temperature rises. Moreover, under the same state of charge (SOC) and temperature conditions, batteries with a higher state of health (SOH) exhibit lower internal resistance compared to those with a lower SOH. During temperature fluctuations, particularly in the range of −20 °C–0 °C, batteries with low SOH show significant instability in their internal resistance. With repeated discharge cycles, the electrode materials in lithium-ion batteries degrade, leading to an increase in the concentration of active compounds in the electrolyte. Byproducts from side reactions gradually accumulate on the electrode surface, obstructing the normal intercalation and deintercalation of lithium ions. As the cycle count increases, the solid electrolyte interface (SEI) layer thickens, further contributing to the rise in internal resistance. During charge and discharge cycles, lithium deposition and detachment reduce the size of mossy lithium layers, which may detach from the electrode substrate under mechanical stress and form electronically inert lithium layers. These layers hinder the diffusion of lithium ions across the electrode surface, thereby increasing internal resistance.

Furthermore, in a battery pack configuration, the compact structure exerts increased mechanical compressive stress on the dead lithium layers, which promotes their formation and further increases internal resistance. Additionally, at lower cathode tortuosity, lithium ions rapidly deintercalate from the cathode and reach the anode surface under high current conditions. However, the ions cannot rapidly intercalate into the anode, leading to significant polarization. Conversely, at higher anode tortuosity, lithium ions cannot efficiently penetrate the anode interior upon reaching the anode surface, leading to increased polarization and higher internal resistance.

To further investigate the entropy-thermal relationships of batteries at different SOH levels, the entropy-thermal coefficients were calculated, and the relationship curves between entropy-thermal coefficients and SOC are presented in Fig. 6 . The entropy-thermal coefficient of the LFP battery typically ranges from −1.6 to 0.3. Overall, high-SOH batteries exhibit a higher entropy-thermal coefficient than low-SOH batteries. At 100 % SOH, the battery reaches its highest entropy-thermal coefficient at 60 % SOC. However, as the battery ages and its SOH decreases, the entropy-thermal coefficient exhibits a positive correlation with SOC, and its maximum value progressively shifts to higher temperatures.

Internal resistance and entropy-thermal coefficient of lithium-ion batteries are both influenced by changes in the battery's SOC and SOH. The internal resistance of the traction battery affects the generation of irreversible heat inside the battery through Joule heating, while the entropy-thermal coefficient reflects the battery's reversible heat efficiency. Both of these factors jointly affect the heat generation and temperature changes of the battery, and both are influenced by variations in the SOC and SOH of the battery. From a physical mechanism perspective, there is no direct correlation between the two, which can be evidenced by the differing impacts of various external factors on each. For example, this study reveals that temperature affects the internal resistance of the battery, while the SOC state has a more significant impact on the entropy-thermal coefficient than temperature. However, both factors can generate an interrelated coupling effect through temperature, SOC, and SOH states. Under different temperature conditions, changes in the internal resistance of the battery will affect temperature variations, thereby influencing the entropy-thermal coefficient. At certain SOC stages, where temperature variations are dominated by internal resistance, these changes may alter the state of the entropy-thermal coefficient, even changing its positive or negative relationship. Additionally, changes in internal resistance affect the temperature variation between charge and discharge, which can impact the aging process of the battery, thus influencing the degradation of the anode and cathode materials and ultimately affecting the entropy-thermal relationship of the battery.

This study investigates the relationship between internal resistance and entropy-thermal coefficients based on the electrochemical reactions and heat generation mechanisms of lithium-ion batteries under varying SOC and temperature conditions. A comparative approach is used to analyze different chemical systems and health states of batteries. The results reveal that the entropy-thermal coefficient is significantly influenced by SOC. Particularly, the entropy-thermal coefficient gradually increases with increasing SOC. At 20 % SOC, the lithium-ion batteries exhibit negative entropy-thermal coefficients. At 40 % SOC, h-NCM and Si–C batteries exhibit negative entropy-thermal coefficients. At around 60 % SOC, the batteries exhibit the highest entropy-thermal coefficient.

Temperature changes have minimal impact on entropy-thermal coefficients. The entropy-thermal coefficients of LFP and m-NCM batteries remain relatively stable across various temperatures and SOC levels. However, the coefficients of h-NCM and Si-C batteries significantly vary with temperature changes. The entropy-thermal coefficients of batteries are influenced by both SOH and SOC. At around 60 % SOC, high-SOH batteries tend to exhibit the highest entropy-thermal coefficient. However, as SOH decreases, the peak coefficient becomes less pronounced and shifts toward higher SOC levels.

In summary, this study presents a novel investigation into the interplay between internal resistance and entropy-thermal coefficients in traction batteries, providing a comprehensive framework for understanding their impact on battery performance and thermal behavior. The experimental analysis, conducted under varying SOC and temperature conditions, highlights the non-linear nature of entropy-thermal coefficients and their significant influence on heat generation and battery efficiency. This work contributes valuable insights into the development of more efficient, durable, and safer battery systems, addressing critical challenges in the growing field of electric vehicles and energy storage applications. Future studies should explore the integration of these models into real-time thermal management systems, further advancing battery technology for sustainable energy solutions.

Tianyi Ma: Writing – original draft, Investigation. Zhipeng Sun: Writing – review & editing, Investigation, Conceptualization. Ce Han: Writing – review & editing, Investigation. Yingzhou Wang: Resources, Investigation. Baoqiang Zhang: Investigation. Jinjie Zhang: Formal analysis, Data curation. Liduo Chen: Investigation. Xiaoqian Dai: Project administration, Methodology. Shijie Zhang: Resources. Shaopeng Shen: Investigation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors appreciate financial supports from National Key R&D Program of China (2022YFE0207300), National Natural Science Foundation of China (62203102), Young Elite Scientists Sponsorship Program by CAST (2021QNRC001), Key R&D Program of Tianjin (24ZYJDSS00140).

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Tianyi Ma, PhD, Senior Engineer. Serves as the Chief Expert at CATARC and Technical Director of the New Energy Inspection Center. His research focuses on multi-level testing methods for traction batteries, including in-situ testing, non-destructive testing, and failure analysis.

Zhipeng Sun, Senior Engineer. Serves as the Test Engineer at CATARC New Energy Vehicle Research and Inspection Center (Tianjin) Co., Ltd. His research focuses on the testing of traction batteries and related non-destructive physical-chemical analysis.

Ce Han, Ph.D., Engineer, serves at China Automotive Technology and Research Center Co., Ltd. His research focuses on testing methods for advanced traction batteries, with a particular emphasis on exploring the relationship between battery materials and their performance characteristics.

Yingzhou Wang, Ph.D., Associate Professor with the School of Automation Engineering, Northeast Electric Power University. His research interests include state and life estimation, thermal effects and fault diagnosis of lithium-ion batteries in energy storage systems, and the application of intelligent algorithms to electronic and electrical systems.

Baoqiang Zhang, Ph.D., Senior engineer in CATARC New Energy Vehicle Research and Inspection Center (Tianjin) Co., Ltd. His main research fields include high-power charging, wireless charging, battery swapping, V2G and other emerging charging technologies for electric vehicles, as well as their testing and evaluation.
Jinjie Zhang, Engineer & Project Manager in CATARC New Energy Vehicle Research and Inspection Center (Tianjin) Co., Ltd. Mainly engaged in testing and evaluation of lithium-ion batteries.

Liduo Chen, Senior Engineer in CATARC New Energy Vehicle Research and Inspection Center (Tianjin) Co., Ltd. His research focuses on multi-level testing methods for traction batteries, including in-safety testing, performance testing, and cyclic utilization.

Xiaoqian Dai, Engineer & Project Manager in CATARC New Energy Vehicle Research and Inspection Center (Tianjin) Co., Ltd. Her research focuses on multi-level testing methods for traction batteries, including electrical performance testing and failure analysis.

Shijie Zhang, Engineer & Project Manager in CATARC New Energy Vehicle Research and Inspection Center (Tianjin) Co., Ltd. His main research focuses on thermal safety mechanisms and testing methods of traction batteries.

Shaopeng Shen, Testing and Validation Engineer in CATARC New Energy Vehicle Research and Inspection Center (Tianjin) Co., Ltd. His research focuses on evaluation technologies for batteries, including thermal characteristics and safety analysis of lithium-ion and sodium-ion batteries.
